coredata.dc:title: Spinel MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (MFO-NPs) for CO2 cyclic decomposition prepared from ferromanganese ores
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanbo
Wang, Jia
Su, Zijian
Lu, Manman
Liu, Shuo
Gu, Foquan
Liu, Jicheng
Tu, Yikang
Jiang, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Ferromanganese ores with high contents of Mn and Fe are abundant worldwide. In this study, spinel-type MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (MFO-NPs) were first synthesized via a novel solid-state reaction process followed by ball-milling, magnetic separation, and nano-grinding. The synthetic MFO-NPs presented a stable spinel structure, fine granularity, and large specific surface; these characteristics highlight their potential in the preparation of thermochemical catalysts. We additionally conducted H2 reduction (HR) and CO2 decomposition reaction (CDR) cyclic tests to investigate the catalytic performance of MFO-NPs. The MFO-NPs showed excellent recyclability and thermal stability during CO2 decomposition; moreover, oxygen-defect MFO-NPs were able to completely decompose CO2 into carbon quantum dots. The mechanism of CO2 decomposition on the MFO-NPs’ surfaces was analysed in detail by XRD, SEM, TEM, TG, Raman, XPS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferromanganese ores
Nano-grinding
MnFe2O4 nanoparticles
Oxygen defects
CO2 decomposition
Carbon quantum dots

CO2 cyclic decomposition properties of the MFO-NPs
Decomposition mechanism of CO2 on the MFO-NPs’ surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: H2 and CO2 consumption curves relative to the HR and CDR processes.
XRD patterns of the MFO and MFO-NPs.
H2-TPR profiles of the MFO and MFO-NPs.
Effect of temperature on the HR-CDR cycles of the MFO-NPs.
TG profile of the MFO-NPs during the HR and CDR processes.
CO2 cyclic decomposition properties of the MFO-NPs (100 cycles).
GC-MS analysis of the outlet gas obtained from the CDR process.
Raman analysis of the HR-CDR products after 100 cycles.
Cumulative carbon content during the CO2 cyclic decomposition test.
TEM analysis of the HR and CDR products after 100 cycles.
(a- TEM image; b- HRTEM image; c- EDS analysis; d, e, f, g, h- EDX elemental mapping images for C, Fe, Mn, and O).
XPS analysis of the raw, HR, and CDR MFO-NPs.
Schematic diagram of the CO2 decomposition mechanism in the MFO-NPs.

head.ce:title: Spinel MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (MFO-NPs) for CO2 cyclic decomposition prepared from ferromanganese ores

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Minerals Processing and Bioengineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Minerals Processing and Bioengineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Minerals Processing and Bioengineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Peace Building, No. 240, School of Minerals Processing & Bioengineering, Central South University, 410083, Changsha, Hunan, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferromanganese ores with high contents of Mn and Fe are abundant worldwide. In this study, spinel-type MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (MFO-NPs) were first synthesized via a novel solid-state reaction process followed by ball-milling, magnetic separation, and nano-grinding. The synthetic MFO-NPs presented a stable spinel structure, fine granularity, and large specific surface; these characteristics highlight their potential in the preparation of thermochemical catalysts. We additionally conducted H2 reduction (HR) and CO2 decomposition reaction (CDR) cyclic tests to investigate the catalytic performance of MFO-NPs. The MFO-NPs showed excellent recyclability and thermal stability during CO2 decomposition; moreover, oxygen-defect MFO-NPs were able to completely decompose CO2 into carbon quantum dots. The mechanism of CO2 decomposition on the MFO-NPs’ surfaces was analysed in detail by XRD, SEM, TEM, TG, Raman, XPS.

ce:section.ce:para: CO2, which is mainly produced from the combustion of fossil fuels, has been regarded as the most important greenhouse gas in the atmosphere [1]. Global warming is considered the major threat to social and economic developments in the 21st century; hence, an increasing number of environmental researchers have focused their efforts on the reduction of CO2 emissions, as well as to the conversion and utilization of this gas [1,2]. Thermochemical, photochemical, biochemical, and electrochemical processes were proposed and applied to the utilization of CO2, in combination with new catalysts and adsorbents [3–6].

Thermochemistry methods are getting attention in view of CO2 reduction; in fact, they are able to decompose CO2 efficiently and completely into carbon without generating any secondary hazardous waste gas (i.e., CH4 or CO) [7–10]. Oxygen-defect magnetite was the first thermochemical catalyst to be applied to CO2 decomposition (in the 1990s). The correspondent value of CO2 decomposition reached 2.9–3.5 mmol/g [7]. Since then, additional binary and ternary modified spinel ferrites (e.g., MFe2O4, M = Ni, Zn, Co, Mn, Cu, Cr, Mg) were taken into consideration [11–15]. The oxygen ions of spinel ferrite (i.e., AB2O4) are packed tightly in a cubic compact packing (CCP) mode; additionally, the metal ions M2+ and Fe3+ fill the tetrahedron (i.e., A site) and octahedral (i.e., B site) gaps, respectively. Oxygen defects (δ), critical for the decomposition of CO2, were easily formed on the surface of MFe2O4-δ under H2 reduction conditions. The high decomposition efficiency of the catalyst was due to the reactivity of M2+ in the A site; in addition, we found that the spinel's chemical stability during the reaction significantly affected recyclability [16].

Magnetic nano spinel ferrites are generally acknowledged as most of the promise materials for environmental governance because of their low cost, nontoxicity, chemical stability and interesting magnetic behaviours [17–19]. MnFe2O4 has a much stable structure than most spinel ferrites, due to the similar atomic radiuses of Mn and Fe [11]. Oxygen-defect MnFe2O4 nanoparticles (MFO-NPs) have an excellent performance when applied to CO2 decomposition [11,20]. Traditional methods used for the preparation of spinel-type ferrite NPs include sol-gel, co-precipitation, and hydrothermal methods [21–24]. However, these methods involve high energy consumption, long reaction times, and a low yield of products [25,26].

MnFe2O4 can be easily obtained from ferromanganese ores through a solid-state reaction method; moreover, it can be separated and recovered effectively by magnetic separation [27–31]. Nano-grinding appears to be a convenient (i.e., simple and low-cost) method for crushing powder material into the nanoscale [32]. The above studies contributed to the development of new ideas on the synthesis of MFO-NPs.

In this study, ferromanganese ores were used as raw materials to synthesize high-purity spinel-type MFO-NPs: a solid-state reaction process was followed by grinding, magnetic separation, and nano-grinding. The MFO-NPs’ behaviour during CO2 cyclic decomposition was investigated by XRD, SEM, BET, H2-TPR, CO2-TPO, TEM, TG, XPS, Raman.

The ferromanganese ores used in this study came from the Jiangxi Province (China) (composition: 30.35 wt% Fe, 14.30 wt% Mn) and the Republic of South Africa (composition: 38.75 wt% Fe, 19.58 wt% Mn). The synthesis flowsheet of the MFO-NPs (Fig. 1 ) shows how the ferromanganese ores were first mixed at a Fe: Mn mole ratio of 2:1, and then finely grounded until 100 wt% was <0.045 mm.

Subsequently, the mixtures were briquetted into 10 × 10 mm cylinders and roasted at 1250 °C for 4 h in air atmosphere [28]. After the samples cooled to room temperature, they were ball-milled and grounded until 100 wt% was <0.045 mm. The magnetic separation of the samples was conducted in a magnetic separator (Model: XCGS-73, Davis magnetic tube) under a magnetic field intensity of 600 Gs. The resulting magnetic concentrated material was spinel MnFe2O4 (MFO). The MFO was wet-milled by nano-grinding it with a Fritsch Planetary Micro Mill (PULVERISETTE 7) at 1100 rpm for 90 min. The grounded product was then separated by centrifugation, obtaining spinel-type MFO-NPs with Fe and Mn contents of 23.75% and 48.28%, respectively.

The phase composition of synthetic product carriers was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Cu-target Bruker D8 Advance). The XRD patterns were collected between 10° and 80° and at scanning rate of 2°/min. The specific surface area of the MFO-NPs was measured by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET, Micromeritics-ASAP 2020), while the samples' morphology was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EDS, Quanta 650 FEG). An X-ray photoelectron spectra analysis (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, 1253.6eV, 150W) was conducted to reveal the chemical shift and valence of elements on the surface; moreover, the microstructure was analysed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 Spirit and JEOL 2100 Cryo). The MFO-NPs’ activity was demonstrated by H2 temperature-programmed reduction (H2-TPR, Micromeritics AutoChem2920), using 10 vol% H2/Ar gas at a flow velocity of 20 ml/min. The hydrogen consumption was monitored using an in-situ thermal conductivity detector (TCD), while the gaseous products were detected by on-line gas phase mass spectrometry (GCMS, QP2010 ULTRA analyser). The speciation of carbon was detected using a Raman spectrometer (Lab RAM HR800, 532 nm, 0.1 mW); were used to measure. The mass changes under varying temperature were determined via thermogravimetric analysis (TG) under H2 and CO2 atmospheres using a TG209F1 Libra (NETZSCH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of H2 reduction and CO2 decomposition
ce:section.ce:para: H2 reduction (HR) and CO2 decomposition reaction (CDR) tests were carried out using a Micromeritics AutoChem2920. The experimental procedure included the following main steps. (1) Fifty mg of sample were placed in a U-type quartz tube reactor, and high-purity argon gas was introduced to drain the air in the reactor for 30 min (2) The temperature rose by 10 °C/min until reaching the target reaction temperature, the flow of argon gas was stopped, and 10 vol% H2/Ar gas was immediately introduced. The HR reaction proceeded under constant temperature. The H2 signal intensities during the reaction are indicated by curves in Fig. 2 -a. (3) After the end of the HR process, the argon gas was introduced again for 30 min; then the CDR process was conducted under 10 vol% CO2/Ar gas. The CO2 signal intensities during the reaction are indicated by curves in Fig. 2-b.

During each test, the total flow rate of the inlet gas was of 20 ml/min and the temperature fluctuated by ±0.1 °C. The consumptions of H2 and CO2 during each HR-CDR cycling test were calculated following Eq. (1):
(1)
∑
( H 2 /CO 2 )
=
∫
t 0
t 1
x d t
l
(
t 1
−
t 0
)
· V · 10 % ·
( t 1 − t 0 )
where Σ is the cumulative consumption of H2 or CO2 (ml), l the baseline difference between the Ar and mixed H2/Ar (1:9) gases or mixed CO2/Ar (1:9) gases, V the gas flow rate (ml/min), t
0 the initial reaction time of the HR or CDR process (min), and t
1 the reaction ending time of the HR or CDR process (min). The cumulative consumption of H2 or CO2 was calculated by applying a normalization method and is represented by the shaded areas in Fig. 2-a and b [33].

As described in Section 2.1 and shown in Fig. 1, we synthesized MnFe2O4 characterized by MFO and MFO-NPs having different particle sizes. All samples were subsequently prepared for XRD, SEM-EDS, BET, and H2-TPR analysis. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the MFO and MFO-NPs products, as well as the standard PDF card of MnFe2O4 (PDF#74–2403).

Both products showed a high purity and a good match with the information on the PDF standard card; moreover, no diffraction peaks associated with impurities were observed. The MFO-NPs’ XRD peak positions and intensities remained almost unchanged, indicating that the spinel structure of MnFe2O4 was stable during the nano-grinding process; however, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) values of the MFO-NPs increased considerably (Fig. 3) due to the changes in grain size. The grain size of the MFO-NPs, calculated by Scherer's formula, was of only 40.5 nm. The results of the SEM-EDS analysis (Fig. 4 ) demonstrate that each MFO-NP presented a uniform particle size; moreover, most particles were individual, near-spherical, irregular, and ranged in size between 50 and 200 nm. Moreover, the EDS analysis indicated that the Fe:Mn atomic ratio was close to the theoretical value for MnFe2O4, highlighting the high purity of the synthetic MFO-NPs. Overall, the results of the chemical and EDS analyses indicate that the MFO-NPs had a theoretical purity of 99.7%.

In order to further investigate the microstructure size of the MFO and MFO-NPs, we conducted BET and laser particle size analyses. The MFO and MFO-NPs had specific surface areas of 13.9 m2/g (median particle diameter D50 = 2.8 μm) and 124.5 m2/g (D50 = 56.3 nm), respectively. These results indicate that the specific surface area of the MFO was efficiently enlarged during the nano-grinding process, approximating that of the nano-MnFe2O4 synthesized in a previous work using the wet-chemical method [34]. Interestingly, a higher specific surface area corresponds a higher number of active sites for gas-solid catalytic reactions to take place.

The H2-TPR analysis can be used effectively to evaluate thermochemical catalysts; therefore, we applied it to investigate the reactivity of the MFO and MFO-NPs [22,23]. Our results are shown in Fig. 5 , together with the TPR curves obtained in previous studies and relative to the nano-MnFe2O4 synthesized through the sol-gel and co-precipitation methods [22,23].

As shown in Fig. 5, the TCD signal was converted to obtain the instantaneous H2 consumption under a certain range of reaction temperatures. The instantaneous H2 consumption is positively correlated with the reaction reactivity: no H2 consumption peaks could be observed for the MFO below 400 °C; however, they increased considerably when the reaction temperature exceeded 500 °C. Notably, two reduction peaks were found in the H2-TPR profile of the MFO-NPs: the highest H2 consumptions were reached at ~ 300 °C and 485 °C, respectively. These values are close or even better than those obtained for the nano-MnFe2O4 in previous studies [11,22,23]. The high H2 consumption values and the low reduction temperatures of the synthetic MFO-NPs prove their excellent activity. Previous studies indicated that the reduction peaks of 300 °C and 485 °C are associated with the formation of oxygen-defect MnFe2O4-δ and the reduction of MnFe2O4 (Eq. (2) and (3)) [7,14,15].
(2) MnFe2O4 + δ·H2(g) = MnFe2O4-δ + δ·H2O(g)
(3) MnFe2O4-δ + (1-δ)·H2(g) = 2·FeO + MnO + (1-δ)·H2O(g)

Moreover, it is known that gas-solid reaction catalysts need to have a stable structure and a high specific surface area [35]. Our XRD and H2-TPR results show that nano-grinding is an efficient mechanical activation process, capable of improving the specific surface of MFO while preserving the spinel structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 cyclic decomposition properties of the MFO-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The results illustrated in Section 3.1 demonstrate that the MFO-NPs prepared from ferromanganese ores had an excellent catalytic activity and may be efficiently used in HR and CDR reactions. Two H2 consumption peaks were identified at 300 °C and 485 °C (Fig. 5); therefore, we decided to investigate the effect of temperature on the CO2 cyclic decomposition properties of the MFO-NPs. The H2 reduction and CO2 decomposition curves for temperatures of 300 °C and 485 °C are shown in Fig. 6 , together with the XRD patterns of the reaction products.

Fig. 6-a and b show obvious H2/CO2 consumption peaks in correspondence of different temperatures. During the first HR-CDR cycle, the consumption of both H2 and CO2 was much lower at 300 °C than that of 485 °C. The profiles remained almost unchanged during the second HR-CDR cycle at 300 °C; however, the HR-CDR consumption was lowered considerably at 485 °C.

The XRD analysis of the reaction products (Fig. 6-c) indicated that the MFO-NPs phase remained similar to that of the original MnFe2O4 after the HR-CDR cycle test at 300 °C; however, it changed considerably at a temperature of 485 °C, due to the generation of new phases of MnO and Fe3O4. A previous study [28] showed that MnO and FeO can be formed through the process described by Eq. (3); subsequently, FeO can be oxidized into Fe3O4, while MnO remains unchanged in a CO2 atmosphere. The results in Fig. 6 demonstrate that the MFO-NPs and oxygen-defect MFO-NPs were cyclically regenerated during the HR-CDR cycles at 300 °C; nevertheless, the spinel structure was destroyed at higher reaction temperatures, hindering the CO2 cyclic decomposition. Based on this data, 300 °C was selected as the most suitable temperature for the succeeding HR-CDR cyclic tests.
The TG analysis was conducted to investigate any changes in the mass of the MFO-NPs during the HR-CDR process. The correspondent results are shown in Fig. 7 .

Fig. 7 shows how the mass decreased continuously, reaching a minimum of 2.20 wt%. This mass loss was attributed to the formation of oxygen-defects (through the process described by Eq. (2)). The value of the oxygen-defect (δ in MnFe2O4-δ = 0.320) was calculated based on Eq. (4):
(4)
δ =
M × △ w
16
where δ is the value of the oxygen-defect in MnFe2O4-δ, M the relative molecular weight of MnFe2O4, Δw the mass loss of the test sample (wt.%), and 16 the relative atomic mass of an oxygen atom.

An obvious mass increase, which reflected the transformation of MnFe2O4-δ into MnFe2O4 during the CO2 oxidation reaction, was detected during the CDR process (Fig. 7). Notably, the final weight of the product after the CDR process was 0.07 wt% higher than those of the raw materials, likely reflecting the formation of additional products.

In order to study the cycle stability of the CO2 cyclic decomposition properties, we performed an uninterrupted HR-CDR test of 100 cycles at 300 °C; the correspondent results are shown in Fig. 8 . The consumption of H2 and CO2 during each test was calculated following Eq. (1) (by a normalization method); then, the value of the oxygen-defect (δ) was calculated based on Eq. (5):
(5)
δ =
Σ ×
10
− 3
× M
m × 22.4
where Σ is the cumulative consumption of H2 (ml), M the relative molecular weight of MnFe2O4, m the mass of the raw sample (g), and 22.4 the ideal gas constant (L/mol).

Fig. 8-a and b show how H2 reduction and CO2 decomposition proceeded rapidly during first 3 min and then slowed down and stopped in about 10 min. The MFO-NPs were highly active during throughout the cyclic tests; moreover, the consumption of both of H2 and CO2 decreased slightly with the cycle times. These results indicate that the oxygen defects of the MFO-NPs can form easily in a H2 atmosphere and recover in a CO2 atmosphere. Hence, efficient CO2 cyclic decompositions can take place during cyclic HR-CDR processes.

Changes in the oxygen-defect value and CO2 consumption during the cycles were calculated following Eqs. (5) and (1). Fig. 8-c shows how the maximum oxygen-defect value (δ) was of 0.323 for the first cycle. This value is similar to that obtained shown in Fig. 7 (0.320), demonstrating the accuracy of the result (error < 1%). The value of δ decreased slightly to 0.281 and 0.241 after 50 and 100 cycles, respectively. Meanwhile, CO2 consumption (Fig. 8-d) decreased from 3.205 mmol/g to 2.340 mmol/g. Overall, we noticed an obvious positive correlation between CO2 consumption and the oxygen-defect value, in accordance with previous studies [11,14,15]. Furthermore, both of the oxygen-defect value and CO2 consumption improved after the cyclic test [14,15]. All of the above results showed that the MFO-NPs have a good performance in CO2 decomposition and excellent recyclability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decomposition mechanism of CO2 on the MFO-NPs’ surface
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the cyclic tests (Fig. 8) proved that CO2 was decomposed efficiently in the presence of oxygen-defect MFO-NPs; however, the nature of the decomposition products was unclear. Previous studies have demonstrated that CO2 can transform into other compounds (e.g., CO, CH4, CH3OH) [7,11,37]. Here, we investigated the decomposition mechanism of CO2 on the surface of the MFO-NPs. To this purpose, we first collected the outlet gas derived from the CDR process and tested it by GC-MS. The correspondent results are shown in Fig. 9 .

Two clear peaks occurred in the mass spectrum of the gas components (Fig. 9-a). The mass spectrum peaks remained almost unchanged until 1.35 min, but the mass spectral peak area gradually increased after 1.6 min. We also analysed the Mass Spectrometry fragment tables of the mass spectral peaks. The reaction gas of the CDR was 10 vol% CO2/Ar, which contained unavoidable impurities of N2 and H2O. The MS fragments of this gas can be factored into CO2 (m/z = 44, 32, 28, 12), Ar (m/z = 40), N2 (m/z = 14, 28), and H2O (m/z = 16, 18). Peak 1 was mainly caused by N2 and Ar (Fig. 9-b), indicating that these two gases were indeed present. The signal intensity (m/z = 44) increased considerably with the reaction time (Fig. 9-c), suggesting that CO2 consumption proceeded rapidly during the first 3 min (Fig. 8-b). No characteristic MS fragments were found for CO (m/z = 12, 16, 28), CH4 (m/z = 16, 15, 14), and CH3OH (m/z = 32, 15). The results of the GC-MS demonstrated that the MFO-NPs did not produce CO during the CDR process, and that CO2 could be transformed into solid products.

Raman analyses can be applied very effectively to the identification of different forms of carbon in solid materials. Here, this technique was used to test the MFO-NPs sample obtained after 100 cycles; Fig. 10 displays the correspondent results. Two broad features, located between 13 407–1450 cm−1 and 1500–1700 cm−1, were associated with the scattering of the D (1350 cm−1) and G (1580 cm−1) peaks [37,38]. The D peak was caused by a disordered scattering of graphite crystals, while the G peak was caused by the hybridization of graphite carbon (sp2). Our results indicated that carbon existed in the sample mainly in the form of amorphous carbon: CO2 was resolved into C during the CDR process (as in Eq. (6)) and the oxygen-defect MFO-NPs were recovered in the form of MnFe2O4.
(6) MnFe2O4-δ + (δ/2)·CO2(g) = MnFe2O4 + (δ/2)·C

The cumulative carbon content during the cyclic HR-CDR tests was calculated based on the CO2 consumption (as in Fig. 8 and Eq. (6)); the correspondent results are shown in Fig. 11 . The amount of decomposed carbon was of ~ 0.008 g/g MFO-NPs, explaining the mass addition shown in Fig. 7; moreover, the cumulative carbon content reached 0.710 g/g MFO-NPs after 100 cycles of HR-CDR.

A TEM analysis was additionally carried out to research possible structure changes of the MFO-NPs after the cyclic tests; the correspondent results are shown in Fig. 12 . The MFO-NPs remained in the form of irregular NPs having an average size of 28.5 ± 5.7 nm (Fig. 12-a). This indicates that the catalysts did not agglomerate during the cyclic HR-CDR process and perhaps caused a decrease of the active specific surface area. Furthermore, an HRTEM image of the NPs (Fig. 12-b) shows obvious lattice fringes that matched well with the (311) (d = 0.25 nm). This result was confirmed by the XRD analysis (Fig. 3): the (311) peak of the spinel-type MnFe2O4 was found at 2θ = 35.21°, indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.25 nm. The EDX results (Fig. 12-c) provided information about the composition of the MFO-NPs. The Mn: Fe atom ratio was close to the theoretical value of 1:2; meanwhile, the carbon content of the products reached 35.2 wt% (a value similar to the calculated cumulative content shown in Fig. 11). The elemental mapping in Fig. 12 d-h showed a homogeneous distribution of Mn, Fe, O, and C in the samples, confirming that the deposition of carbon at the atomic scale. In addition, Fig. 12 shows that carbon particles (average size = ~1 nm) was homogeneously distributed on the surface of the MFO-NPs. Nevertheless, the nanocarbon dots generated from the decomposition of CO2 formed only on the surface of the MFO-NPs, and the accumulation of solid carbon decreased the effective area for the gas-solid catalytic reactions. Thus, the CO2 cyclic decomposition ability of the MFO-NPs decreased gradually as the number of cycles (Fig. 8). Notably, the morphology and distribution of carbon in our samples was much similar to that of carbon quantum dots [38,39]. The potential of the new MnFe2O4/C nanocomposite material in the preparation of photoelectric functional materials was further investigated as follows.
XPS was used to check the chemical state of Mn, Fe, O in the raw, HR, and CDR MFO-NPs; the correspondent results are shown in Fig. 13 .

An obvious increase of the C1s photoelectron peak was observed for the CDR MFO-NPs (Fig. 13-a). The corresponding binding energy (284.8 eV) was closer to that of the C–C bonds (284.8 eV) rather than to that of the C–O (286.6 eV) and CO (288.6 eV) bonds [39]. Our XPS analysis clarified that CO2 was decomposed into solid carbon (following Eq. (6)), in accord with the results of the Raman and TEM analyses. The binding energies of the MFO-NPs (indicated as Fe2p and Mn2p) were obtained from previous works [40,41]. Fig. 13-b and c showed the Fe 2p and Mn 2p photoelectron spectra of the raw, HR, and CDR MFO-NPs. Notably, the binding energies of the Fe2p3/2 were slightly different (710.90 eV, 710.85 eV, and 710.87 eV, respectively). Meanwhile, the peak of Mn2p3/2 showed a remarkable decrease from 641.42 eV to 641.15 eV after the H2 reduction, reaching a value of 641.50 eV during the CDR process. These results reveal the reversibility of the electron transfer process that occurred during the HR-CDR cycles, and which interested mainly the Mn atom.

The O1s high-resolution XPS spectra in Fig. 13-d showed obvious peaks. Peak I corresponded to a binding energy of 529.7 eV and was caused by the presence of lattice oxygen in the MFO spinel. Peak III, which corresponded to a binding energy of 532.6 eV, was particularly obvious in the raw MFO-NPs and related to the adsorbed oxygen of CO2 or H2O. Peak II, which corresponded to a binding energy of 530.9 eV, reflected the occurrence of oxygen defects [42–45]. Notably, Peak III almost disappeared after the HR process, while the area of Peak II increased considerably. These data indicate that the oxygen defects formed on the surface of the MFO-NPs (following the process described by Eq. (2)). Furthermore, the area of Peak II decreased significantly after the CDR process, indicating that the oxygen defects of the MFO-NPs were partial oxidized (following the process described by Eq. (6)).

Based on the above analysis, we created a schematic diagram of the CO2 decomposition mechanism occurring on the surface of the MFO-NPs during the cyclic HR-CDR process (Fig. 14 ). MnFe2O4 has a typical AB2O4 spinel-type structure. There, the oxygen atoms are closely packed in a Face Centre Cubic (FCC) configuration, the Fe3+ ions are located in octahedral sites (B site) and hexa-coordinated with the adjacent O2−, and the tetra-coordinated Mn2+ ions are located in tetrahedral sites (A site). During the HR process, the O2− at the MFO surface can react with the adsorbed H2, forming H2O through the process described by Eq. (2); subsequently oxygen defects can form. In keeping the electrical neutrality of the MFO, Mn2+ is more likely to lose electrons than Fe3+ (Fig. 13), because Fe3+ has a higher coordination number and a lower binding energy [11]. During the CDR process, we introduced CO2 gas, causing the oxidation of electron-deficient Mn and the filling of oxygen defects with the O2− derived from the CO2. Hence, the oxygen-defect MFO gradually recovered, becoming a normal MFO. Meanwhile, the CO2 was reduced to amorphous carbon (Eq. (6)) and distributed on the surface of the MFO. Notably, the spinel structure remained stable during the HR-CDR process, contributing to the cycling stability.

ce:list-item.ce:para: In this study, we developed a novel process for the preparation of MFO-NPs from ferromanganese ores by mean of oxidation roasting, ball-milling, magnetic separation, and nano-grinding processing. We hence obtained MFO-NPs having a purity of 99.7%, a median particle diameter of 56.3 nm, and a specific surface area of 124.5 m2/g. These synthetic MFO-NPs had a high catalytic activity; moreover, 300 °C was selected as the most suitable temperature for the subsequent CO2 decomposition reaction.

A maximum oxygen-defect value of 0.323 was registered during the H2 reduction process. After that, 3.205 mmol/g of CO2 were decomposed on the surface of oxygen-defect MnFe2O4-δ. The MFO-NPs demonstrated an excellent recyclability for CO2 decomposition; notably, their efficiency remained >70% after 100 cycles.

The H2 reduction and CO2 decomposition mechanism that occurred on the surface of the MFO-NPs could be expressed as follows: MnFe2O4 + δ·H2(g) = MnFe2O4-δ + δ·H2O and MnFe2O4-δ + (δ/2)·CO2(g) = MnFe2O4 + (δ/2)·C. During the HR-CDR process, the formation of oxygen defects played a crucial role in the decomposition of CO2. Finally, the CO2 was completely decomposed into amorphous carbon dots having an average size of ~ 1 nm.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests. No experiments on live vertebrates and/or higher invertebrates were performed in this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1960114), the Central South University Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (No. 2018zzts780), and the Support by the Open Sharing Fund for the Large-scale Instruments and Equipment of Central South University (No. CSUZC201907).
